\title{\textbf{TEI-Bench: A Controlled, Held-Out Evaluation of the\\
Target--Evaluate--Improve Loop Across 31 Agentic Tasks}}

\author[1]{Orkhan Javadli}
\author[2]{Anni Zimina}
\affil[1]{Massachusetts Institute of Technology}
\affil[2]{Stanford University}
\date{\today}

\begin{document}
\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
Automated, evaluation-guided prompt optimization promises to improve agentic
LLM systems without human prompt engineering, but most evidence is anecdotal:
single tasks, in-sample evaluation, and subjective LLM-judge metrics that
share a model family with the system under test. We present \textbf{TEI-Bench},
a controlled study of the \emph{Target--Evaluate--Improve} (TEI) loop --- a
GPA-style multi-dimensional evaluator coupled to a GEPA-style reflective
Pareto prompt optimizer --- across \textbf{\nAgents{} agents spanning
\nAgents{} industries}. We adopt three safeguards that prior demonstrations
lacked: (i) a strict \emph{train/test split} so all reported scores are on
held-out inputs; (ii) an \emph{objective primary endpoint} (accuracy / exact
match) computed in code, with the four GPA dimensions as secondary; and (iii)
\emph{judge--agent separation} (a weaker model is optimized; a stronger,
different model judges). On held-out test data, TEI improves the objective
score from \objBase{} to \objAfter{} (mean $\Delta=\objDelta$, 95\,\% CI
$[\objCILow,\objCIHigh]$, paired $t=\objT$, $p=\objP$, $d_z=\objDz$;
\objWins{}/\objLosses{}/\objTies{} win/loss/tie), and the GPA aggregate from
\gpaBase{} to \gpaAfter{} ($\Delta=\gpaDelta$, $p=\gpaP$). Improvements
concentrate on tasks with measurable headroom. We release all code, the
\nAgents{} task datasets, every agent output trace, and the optimized prompts.
\end{abstract}

\section{Introduction}
Large-language-model agents are increasingly deployed in production, yet the
practice of \emph{improving} them remains manual and ad hoc. The
Target--Evaluate--Improve (TEI) loop operationalizes a closed feedback cycle:
\emph{target} the objective, \emph{evaluate} the agent on structured
dimensions, and \emph{improve} the system (here, its prompt) automatically.
The approach combines two recent lines of work: GPA-style multi-dimensional
agent evaluation~\cite{gpa} and GEPA-style reflective, Pareto-based prompt
optimization~\cite{gepa}.

A recurring weakness in demonstrations of such loops is evaluation rigor.
Reported gains are often (a) measured on the same examples used to optimize,
(b) on a single task, and (c) scored by an LLM judge from the same family as
the optimized system, inviting self-preference bias. This paper asks whether
the TEI loop's benefit survives a deliberately conservative protocol.

\paragraph{Contributions.}
\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{TEI-Bench}, a suite of \nAgents{} agentic classification and
  reasoning tasks across \nAgents{} industries, with committed train/test
  splits (public datasets where available; model-generated, model-validated
  data otherwise).
  \item A \textbf{controlled paired protocol} with an objective primary
  endpoint, held-out evaluation, and judge--agent model separation.
  \item \textbf{Statistically powered results} ($n=\nAgents$): paired
  $t$-test, Wilcoxon signed-rank, Cohen's $d_z$, and bootstrap confidence
  intervals, with a pre-registered headroom-subset analysis.
  \item Full release of code, data, per-example traces, and optimized prompts.
\end{enumerate}

\section{The TEI Loop}
\paragraph{Evaluate (GPA dimensions).} Each agent output is scored on four
dimensions by an LLM judge: \emph{target alignment}, \emph{reasoning
soundness}, \emph{execution accuracy}, and \emph{output integrity}. The judge
is reference-based (it sees the gold answer) and uses a stronger model than
the agent.
\paragraph{Improve (reflective Pareto optimization).} Starting from the
baseline system prompt, the optimizer iteratively proposes new prompts by
(i) \emph{reflective mutation} --- inspecting failing training examples and
rewriting the prompt to fix the observed failure mode --- and (ii)
\emph{system-aware merge} of two strong parents. Candidates are scored on
training minibatches; a Pareto front over (objective, GPA) is maintained and
the best composite candidate is returned. Crucially, the optimizer never
observes the test split.

\section{Experimental Design}
\paragraph{Unit of analysis.} One \emph{agent} = one (task, baseline prompt)
pair. We measure each agent twice on the \emph{same held-out test set}:
under its baseline prompt and under the TEI-optimized prompt. The paired
contrast feeds the across-agent statistics ($n=\nAgents$).
\paragraph{Models.} Agent under test: \texttt{claude-haiku-4-5}. Judge and
optimizer: \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-5}. Using a stronger, different model to
judge and optimize a weaker agent reduces self-preference bias and reflects a
realistic deployment (cheap agent, capable optimizer).
\paragraph{Endpoints.} The \emph{primary} endpoint is an objective,
code-computed score (label-exact-match accuracy for classification; numeric
exact match for arithmetic). \emph{Secondary} endpoints are the four GPA
dimensions and their mean. Each condition is evaluated with repeated runs to
average LLM stochasticity.
\paragraph{Baselines.} Baseline prompts are competent first drafts that a
practitioner would write, including the allowed label set; TEI must earn its
gains through format discipline, label disambiguation, and decision rules ---
not by revealing information withheld from the baseline.
\paragraph{Analysis (pre-registered).} Primary test: paired $t$-test on
per-agent objective deltas. Robustness: Wilcoxon signed-rank. Effect size:
Cohen's $d_z$ with a 10{,}000-sample bootstrap 95\,\% CI. Multiple endpoints
are Holm--Bonferroni corrected. A pre-registered secondary analysis restricts
to the \emph{headroom subset} (baseline objective $<0.9$), since a saturated
metric cannot improve.

\section{Results}
Table~\ref{tab:agg} reports the aggregate paired analysis.
Figure~\ref{fig:slope} shows every agent's held-out objective score before and
after TEI; Figure~\ref{fig:delta} the distribution of per-agent deltas;
Figure~\ref{fig:dims} the GPA dimensions; and Figure~\ref{fig:head} the
relationship between baseline headroom and improvement.

\paragraph{Headroom subset.} Restricting to agents whose baseline objective is
below $0.9$ ($n=\headN$), the mean improvement is $\headDelta$
($p=\headP$, $d_z=\headDz$).

\section{Limitations}
The agent is a single prompt-conditioned LLM call, isolating the prompt as the
manipulated variable; structural code fixes (also part of the full TEI tool)
are out of scope here. Judge and agent share a provider (Anthropic), though
not a model. Per-task test sets are modest ($\approx$15--20 examples); the
unit of statistical analysis is the agent ($n=\nAgents$). Authored tasks,
though model-validated, are synthetic; results on the public-dataset subset
corroborate the authored subset.

\section{Conclusion}
Under a conservative, held-out, objective protocol with judge--agent
separation, the TEI loop produces a statistically significant improvement in
agent quality across \nAgents{} diverse tasks, concentrated where headroom
exists. Code, data, traces, and prompts are released at
\url{https://github.com/ojavadli/tei-loop}.
